title: Police prevent potential impaired driving incident
content:
On Tuesday, May 5, 2026, Barrie Police officers attended a residence in the south end of the city regarding unwanted individuals at the property.
Through the investigation, officers interacted with an adult male holding a can of beer who was displaying signs of being impaired, including slurred speech, bloodshot eyes, and uncooperative behaviour. After police advised the man he was required to leave the residence, officers observed him getting into the driver’s seat of a parked vehicle.
Concerned he would drive off while under the influence of alcohol, the officers intervened and placed him under arrest for impaired operation.
The accused was transported to Barrie Police Headquarters where a qualified breath technician confirmed he was over the legal alcohol limit.
As a result, the 59-year-old man from Barrie was charged with:
Operation while impaired by alcohol
Operation while impaired – blood alcohol concentration over 80 mg
Additionally, his driver’s licence was suspended for 90-days, and the vehicle was seized for seven days as required by law.
Barrie Police remind motorists that if you plan to consume alcohol or drugs, you cannot get behind the wheel of a vehicle, even one that is parked. We remain committed to ensuring the safety of everyone on our roadways.
Plan ahead. Stay safe.
Occurrence # BA26028195

title: $100,000 reward highlights renewed push in unsolved Barrie homicide
content:
Homicide Investigations
,
Sometimes the smallest detail can have the biggest impact, providing the break investigators working a cold case have long been waiting for.
The Barrie Police Service Major Crime/Homicide Unit is renewing its appeal for information in the unsolved murder of April Dobson, with a reward now totaling $100,000 for any details that lead to the arrest and conviction of those responsible.
Although more than 20 years have passed since Oct. 14, 2005, when the 40-year-old mother was fatally shot on the front porch of a friend’s home on Browning Trail, the pain of not knowing what happened remains fresh for her family and friends. While life moves on for many, the uncertainty, grief, and questions left by this tragedy linger every day for those closest to her.
Investigators are determined to bring them answers. Even the smallest piece of information could make a real difference in the case.
Detectives are asking the public to think back and consider whether they were in the Browning Trail or Bronte Crescent area that night, noticed any unusual behaviour, or observed someone they know acting differently around that time. Even something that may have seemed unimportant or was dismissed years ago could now be the key to solving this case.
Investigators urge anyone with information, no matter how small it may seem, to come forward – it could prove critical to solving the case.
Police continue to seek information about a young man seen leaving the area on a mountain-style bicycle shortly after the shooting. This person of interest could hold important details. Detectives ask anyone who may have seen him or knows him to contact investigators. Investigators would still like to speak to this male, and believe he may have critical information.
“This case has remained unsolved for far too long, and April’s family deserves answers,” said Sgt. Brett Carleton with the Barrie Police Service Major Crime/Homicide Unit. “Our commitment to solving this case has never wavered. We are considering all possibilities when looking at who may have been involved, and this has remained an active investigation. If you have information that might help us, finally bring closure to a family who has been living with unanswered questions for far too long – please speak up.”
Anyone with information is asked to contact the Barrie Police Service Major Crime/Homicide Unit at 705-725-7025 extension 2160, or anonymously through Crime Stoppers at 1-800-222-TIPS (8477) or
online
.

title: Pedestrian faces slew of drug-related charges after disrupting traffic
content:
On the afternoon of Thursday, May 7, 2026, an officer patrolling in the area of Wellington and Bayfield Streets observed a pedestrian cross the road without using a crosswalk causing vehicles to halt as he tried to avoid oncoming traffic.
When the officer stopped to speak with him, he started to jog into a parking lot on Bayfield Street. The pedestrian was ordered to stop and when he turned around, the officer recognized him as having an outstanding warrant.
While placing the pedestrian under arrest, the officer observed a large golf ball-sized quantity of suspected cocaine in plain view in the man’s sweater pocket. Further investigation led to the discovery of suspected methamphetamine, drug paraphernalia and a quantity of Canadian currency.
The 36-year-old man from Barrie is charged with the following offences:
Possession of Schedule I Substance – Cocaine
Possession of Schedule I Substance – Opioid (other than heroin)
Possession of Schedule I Substance – Methamphetamine
Possession of Schedule I Substance for the Purpose of Trafficking – Cocaine
Possession of Schedule I Substance for the Purpose of Trafficking – Opioid (other than heroin)
Possession of Schedule I Substance for the Purpose of Trafficking – Methamphetamine
Possession of Proceeds of Property Obtained by Crime under $5,000
Pedestrian Fail to use Crosswalk
Fail to comply with Probation Order x2
The investigation is ongoing.

content:
Investigators with the Barrie Police Crimes Against Persons Unit have charged a 28-year-old man in connection with a sexual assault case involving a child.

title: Understanding Barrie Police Service response to calls involving a person in crisis
content:
Not every call for service looks the same. When someone is in crisis, whether due to emotional distress, mental health, or challenges related to substance abuse, Barrie Police prioritize safety, de-escalation, and getting the person the right support. Every call is unique to those involved and requires compassion, understanding, and a measured, informed response.
The Barrie Police Service Mobile Crisis Response Team (MCRT) pairs a uniformed officer with a crisis worker from the Canadian Mental Health Association (CMHA). Together, they respond to calls involving individuals who may not otherwise reach out for help or those desperate for intervention.
“Our approach isn’t enforcement first. We slow things down, we take the time to listen, and we try to understand what’s going on so we can help the person in need. The partnership between MCRT and CMHA allows for that balanced approach, combining policing with mental health expertise.” – Const. Rebecca Henderson with the MCRT Unit.
Many Barrie Police officers are trained in crisis intervention and attend any call where there are safety risks, but having a CMHA worker on scene adds another layer of support and helps to ensure the response fits the situation.
“Essentially, we bring the initial supports to a person in crisis rather than bringing a person in crisis to the supports.” – Const. Rebecca Henderson with the MCRT Unit.
And that partnership extends beyond the scene.
“We take protecting the dignity of individuals in crisis very seriously. We encourage the public and our media partners to avoid sharing identifying details during these difficult moments when someone is at their lowest point to prevent further distress.” – Const. Rebecca Henderson with the MCRT Unit.
Through collaboration efforts, Barrie Police are focused on ensuring that every crisis call is met with the appropriate response, emphasizing support, and connection to care.
If you or someone you know is experiencing a crisis, please reach out to CMHA Simcoe County’s 24/7 Crisis Support Line at 705-728-5044 or 1-800-893-8333.

title: Police seek to locate pickup truck in fail-to-remain collision
content:
,
Traffic
Barrie Police are seeking the public’s assistance in locating a pickup truck involved in a fail-to-remain collision at the GO Transit station on Yonge Street South in the City of Barrie.
On April 29, 2026, around 7:30 a.m., a dark-coloured pickup truck struck a parked vehicle in the station parking lot. The truck remained in the area briefly before leaving the scene. The driver has not been identified.
Investigators believe there were several bystanders in the area at the time who may have witnessed or heard the collision. Police are also hoping to speak with the occupants of a light-coloured car that was positioned directly behind the pickup truck when the incident occurred.
While attempting to exit a parking space, the pickup truck struck the front driver’s side of a grey, four-door KIA that was situated beside it, causing significant damage, including to the bumper and front panel. Investigators believe the pickup truck is a newer model Dodge Ram, possibly black in colour.
The pickup truck will have damage to the passenger side rear door and rear panel over the tire.
Anyone who witnessed the incident or who may have information that could assist in identifying the vehicle or driver is asked to contact police via email at
[email protected]
.

title: Barrie Police Marine Unit conducts first water rescue of the season
content:
On Sunday, May 3, 2026, the Barrie Police Marine Unit was deployed for the first time this season following a report of a woman on a paddleboard in distress on Kempenfelt Bay.
Marine officers attended the area of Minet’s Point Park and could see the female in the middle of the bay being pulled further out by strong currents. She was seen repeatedly falling from her paddleboard into the chilly water. A witness reported that the woman appeared to have had been struggling while attempting to kite surf for more than two hours.
Officers were able to reach the woman a short time later and safely bring her back to solid ground.
The 28-year-old woman, who is visiting from Nova Scotia, was in good health, though perhaps a little exhausted. She declined any medical attention and went on her way following a safety talk from police.
With the warmer weather upon us, Barrie Police remind residents and visitors to check weather and water conditions before heading out, ensure they have proper safety equipment, and avoid entering the water alone, especially in unfamiliar lakes.
Occurrence #BA26027739

title: Barrie Police focus on downtown safety with balanced approach
content:
Barrie Police Service recognizes the growing concern from residents and business owners regarding your sense of safety on our downtown streets.
We hear your concerns and we share them; however, what is being seen is complex. While homelessness, drug use, addiction, and mental health challenges are not unique to the City of Barrie, our approach has to be unique to our community to ensure everyone feels safe.
We are actively responding through increased patrols and a more visible presence downtown. Officers are focused on maintaining public safety while also recognizing not every situation is a criminal matter.
“What you’re often seeing isn’t always crime, it’s people dealing with addiction, mental health struggles, and homelessness. In these circumstances, policing looks different.  Our approach balances compassion with accountability. We are committed to keeping our city and all of its residents safe.” – Chief Rich Johnston
Officers on patrol downtown will take a firm and consistent approach to criminal activity, which includes illicit drug use in public spaces and drug trafficking. When Barrie Police observe illegal behaviours, appropriate action will be taken.
“Our officers are clear in their role. If we see it, we will seize it, and we will take the necessary steps to stop it. There is no easy solution, but our goal is to ensure people feel safe in their community and confident with what Barrie Police are doing.” – Deputy Chief Rob Burke
However, enforcement alone will not solve the issue long-term.
Many individuals seen in distress require medical, social, and housing supports. Barrie Police officers work closely with community partners to connect people with those services whenever possible.
In addition to year-round patrols, the Downtown Engagement Team and City Guardians will provide an enhanced presence during the busy summer months, focusing on proactive engagement, visibility, and support.
Barrie Police work with municipal and provincial partners to address illegal cannabis and psilocybin storefronts operating in the city. These businesses are subject to investigation and enforcement, however, investigations such as these take time and focus on compliance and appropriate enforcement action.
“Together, with the community services that are available and the City of Barrie, there is a concentrated effort to address concerns about downtown safety for everyone to enjoy all that this beautiful city has to offer.” – Deputy Chief Rob Burke
“We are officers of the law and committed to ensuring everyone’s safety, but we are also your neighbours. We live here, we are raising our families here, and we want the same sense of safety as you for our kids, our grandkids, and our community. When our officers are on duty, they’re not just responding to calls – they see people in difficult moments and do their best to help. This approach matters to us, not just professionally, but personally.” – Chief Rich Johnston
The Barrie Police Service is committed to supporting those in need while taking enforcement action when behaviour poses a risk to public safety. Residents are encouraged to report criminal activity and continue to share their concerns. Community plays an important role in shaping ongoing efforts to keep Barrie safe.

title: Two suspects arrested following incident involving firearms
content:
A weapons-related call in a Kozlov Street parking lot turned into a high-risk takedown and two arrests over the weekend.
Barrie Police received a report about a violent disturbance involving two men and two unknown male victims in a north end parking lot at 7:50 p.m., on Sunday, April 26, 2026.
Officers arrived and within six minutes they located the suspect vehicle exiting the commercial plaza and heading onto Bayfield Street. As part of the weapons investigation, police conducted a high-risk stop of the vehicle and detained the four occupants.
One individual matching the suspect description was arrested and transported to Barrie Police Headquarters. The other three vehicle occupants were released unconditionally.
Around 9:30 p.m., officers attending an unrelated incident located an individual matching the second suspect’s description and took him into custody.
Despite extensive searches of the area no firearms were recovered.
After speaking with witnesses and gathering evidence, including video footage, police confirmed the two accused had been in possession of handguns, which were pointed at the two victims.
As a result of the investigation, two 21-year-old men, both from Barrie, are charged with:
Possession of a weapon for a dangerous purpose
Pointing a firearm
Unauthorized possession of a prohibited or restricted firearm
Possession of a firearm or ammunition contrary to prohibition order
Failing to comply with probation order
The investigation is ongoing.
Police encourage the two male victims from the Kozlov Street parking lot incident to contact investigators via email at
[email protected]
.
Additionally, anyone with information on this investigation is encouraged to contact Barrie Police at 705-725-7025.
Occurrence #BA26026180

content:
,
Traffic
Barrie Police officers conducting traffic enforcement on Ferndale Drive North on Thursday, April 23, 2026, arrested a motorist after observing a vehicle dangerously speeding more than double the posted limit.
Officers conducted the traffic stop north of Tiffin Street just after 2 p.m. after radar measured the vehicle travelling 105 km/h in the posted 50 km/h zone. Police also learned the driver was behind the wheel with a suspended licence.
As a result, the 41-year-old man from Barrie is facing charges under the Highway Traffic Act:
Stunt driving – excessive speed
Speeding 105 km/h in a 50 km/h zone

Additionally, the accused was handed a 30-day licence suspension, and the vehicle was impounded for 14 days.
Highway speeds have no place on city streets. Speed limits aren’t suggestions – they are the law to keep everyone safe.

content:

content:
,
Traffic
On Saturday, April 11, 2026, Barrie Police officers patrolling the downtown core spotted a vehicle travelling the wrong way down Clapperton Street, which is a one-way street for northbound traffic.

Impaired operation
Impaired operation – 80 above
Fail to comply with probation order
As a result of the charges, the accused was also given a 90-day driver’s licence suspension, and the vehicle was seized for seven days as required by law.

Barrie Police encourage the public to call 911 if you suspect a driver is impaired.

title: Barrie Police Service launches Next Generation 9-1-1
content:
The Barrie Police Service has officially launched Next Generation 9-1-1 (NG9-1-1), enhancing emergency communications and response times for the community.
NG9-1-1 represents a multi-stage transformative upgrade to the traditional 9-1-1 network. While the process for callers to connect with a 9-1-1 dispatcher remains the same, the technology driving the network offers several improvements:
Faster access to accurate caller location and phone number data for emergency dispatchers
Increased reliability and cyber security
Additional backup capabilities in the event of a network failure
Potential for real-time text and live emergency video calls in the future
The Ontario government has provided more than $2.6 million since 2022 to support the Barrie Police Service’s transition to NG9-1-1.
“Upgrading Ontario’s public safety communications will be a game changer,” said Hon. Michael Kerzner, Solicitor General of Ontario. “Next Generation 9-1-1 gives first responders real-time, life-saving information which means a faster, more effective emergency response. Under the leadership of Premier Ford, our government will always have the backs of those who protect our province. That’s why we are proud to provide funding to Barrie Police Service for this new technology which will mean more calls will be answered, more lives will be saved and people across the city can count on emergency services when it matters most.”
NG9-1-1 will strengthen emergency response by improving the quality and speed of information shared with dispatchers and helping first responders better assess situations before arriving on scene.
“These upgrades are an important step forward for our community,” said Monique Boucher, Communications Administrator with Barrie Police Service. “The new network will allow the public to give our operators more detailed information in an emergency, helping both police and fire respond more effectively. These improvements won’t change how residents contact 9-1-1 today—they can continue to call the emergency line as usual.”
“The Barrie Police Service welcomes these technology upgrades as an important step in strengthening emergency response capabilities and continuing to provide the highest level of service to the community,” stated Barrie Police Chief Rich Johnston.
“Next Generation 9‑1‑1 represents another important step to better serve our residents in emergencies—when seconds matter,” noted Deputy Fire Chief Eric MacFadden.

title: Shooting in Barrie’s south end under investigation

title: Barrie man faces charges in child exploitation investigation
content:
On April 8, 2026, the Barrie Police Service Internet Child Exploitation Unit (ICE) arrested and charged a 29-year-old man from Barrie with possession of child sexual abuse and exploitation material, and accessing child sexual abuse and exploitation material following a four-month investigation.
The ICE Unit received a report from the National Centre of Missing and Exploited Children concerning the upload of child sexual abuse and exploitation material.

title: Cryptocurrency scams take many forms, but share one common goal
content:
Fraud Alert
,
Police are reminding the public to remain vigilant as cryptocurrency-related scams continue to increase, resulting in significant financial losses for victims. In Barrie, cryptocurrency scams have resulted in the loss of more than $3 million from multiple victims in recent months.
“Victims were responding to a video advertisement they had seen on trusted websites such as Google, Microsoft Network and Instagram. The video is often misrepresentation of a known public figure and will encourage you to make a small initial investment. This investment appears to grow at an unusually rapid pace, which allows victims to believe that they can make money quickly. The investment company’s funds may frequently match your initial investment; for example, they may match your $1,000 initial investment with $1,000 of their own funds,” explained Det. Kris Nicholson with the Barrie Police Service Fraud Unit.
“This leads to unusual communication pathways you might not be used to, such as WhatsApp and Signal. The phone numbers that are used to communicate with you are unreliable and untraditional and make it very hard for police to trace. The money is deposited into an online platform that is the shell of a real financial platform, and no money is ever able to be withdrawn,” Det. Nicholson added.
Cryptocurrency scams can take many forms, but they all share a common goal: convincing individuals to transfer digital currency or money under false pretenses.
These scams often begin online, through social media platforms, investment advertisements, messaging apps, or unsolicited emails and text messages. In some cases, victims are contacted by a well-known public figure, such as political figures, corporate public figures, someone claiming to be an investment advisor, financial expert, or even a new online acquaintance. Scammers may promote cryptocurrency as a fast, low-risk way to make money and often use professional-looking websites, charts, and testimonials to appear legitimate.
There are several warning signs the public should be aware of, including promises of guaranteed or unusually high returns, pressure to act quickly, requests for secrecy, and instructions to move conversations off public platforms to private messaging apps. Unsolicited investment offers, especially from individuals you have never met in person, should always be treated with caution.
To reduce the risk of becoming a victim, we encourage residents to independently verify investment opportunities, avoid clicking on suspicious links, and never share passwords or personal banking information.
“It is best to seek your own investment purchases and opportunities by contacting a trusted financial advisor,” Nicholson noted.
Legitimate financial institutions will never ask for cryptocurrency payments or request sensitive information through unsolicited messages.
Trust your gut. If it sounds too good to be true, it probably is.
If you suspect you may be a target of fraud, or if you have already sent funds, don’t be embarrassed – you’re not alone. If you have lost money through fraud, you can call the Barrie Police Service Fraud Unit at 705-725-7025, or
.
If you want to report an attempted fraud, or if you need more information, contact The
Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre.
Staying informed and cautious remains one of the most effective ways to prevent cryptocurrency-related fraud.

title: Fraud investigation leads to charges and counterfeit currency seizure
content:
Fraud Alert
,
Barrie Police have arrested and charged two individuals following an investigation that launched late last year.
In December 2025, police received a report about a private business sale transaction involving a large cash payment described as a down payment. • s.449 CC Making Counterfeit Currency
A 32-year-old female from Whitby, ON, has been charged with:
• s.450(b) CC Possession of Counterfeit Money (x2)

title: Man faces several charges in break-and-enter investigation
content:
Barrie police arrested a man believed to be linked to multiple break-and-enters and thefts in the east end of the city.
On Wed., March 25, 2026, police responded to a report of a break-in and theft at a Eugenia Street residence believed to have taken place in the early morning hours. The homeowner reported receiving an alert from their bank around 4 a.m. about suspicious activity on their credit card. Upon further inspection, the homeowner noticed someone had entered the garage and stolen a wallet containing the credit card from inside one of the vehicles.
The officers learned the credit card had been used to make a purchase at a convenience store on St. Vincent Street later that same morning. Additional transactions were declined by the financial institution due to suspected fraud.
Officers reviewed surveillance footage showing a male suspect inside the store between 4:25 a.m. and 4:35 a.m., during which time the stolen credit card was used.
In the early morning hours of Fri., March 27, 2026, officers were in the area of St. Vincent Street responding to an unrelated call for service when they located an individual matching the suspect description and wearing clothing consistent with that seen in the convenience store surveillance footage.
The suspect was arrested without incident and transported to Barrie Police Service Headquarters.
A 28-year-old Barrie man faces the following charges under the Criminal Code of Canada:
Break, enter a dwelling house with the intent to commit an indictable offence
Theft under $5,000
Fraud under $5,000
Use of credit card – Section 342(1)(c)
Possession of property obtained by crime under $5,000
Investigators believe the accused may be connected to additional incidents involving thefts from residences, vehicles, garages, or sheds specifically in the city’s east end, including the areas of Grove Street, Duckworth Street, St. Vincent Street, and Blake Street.
Residents are asked to review home security or dash camera footage captured during the early morning hours of March 25 through March 27, 2026, and report any suspicious activity or persons. Anyone with information or video footage that may assist investigators is asked to contact the investigating officer via email at
[email protected]
.
Residents are reminded to ensure their vehicle doors are locked, valuables are removed or secured, and garage entry points are properly secured to help prevent opportunistic thefts.

title: Three suspects arrested days after downtown shooting
content:
The Barrie Police Service has made three arrests and laid multiple charges following a shooting in the city’s downtown during the early morning hours of Sun., April 5, 2026.
On Tues., April 7, 2026, members of the Barrie Police Tactical Support Unit attended a residence on Fitton Street in Midland, Ont. and took three individuals into custody without incident. A search warrant executed at the residence resulted in the seizure of a loaded .40 calibre handgun believed to be connected to the shooting.
Police are not looking for any other suspects.
A 30-year-old Barrie man was shot at close range following an altercation involving several people outside a downtown night club. The victim was rushed to a local hospital and then transferred to a Toronto area trauma centre in serious but stable condition. He is currently recovering in hospital.
Through the investigation, Barrie Police officers worked around the clock to locate evidence and identify those responsible. Police recovered a spent shell casing from the scene and projectile that had gone into a glass window of a local establishment across the street from the shooting. Investigators also carefully combed through CCTV footage of the downtown area, which assisted in advancing the investigation.
Barrie Police wish to thank the Ontario Provincial Police Underwater Search and Recovery Unit for its assistance in searching Kempenfelt Bay and recovering evidence.
A 20-year-old Barrie man is facing a series of charges:
Attempt to commit murder using a restricted firearm or prohibited firearm
Aggravated assault
Discharge a restricted firearm or a prohibited firearm with intent
Possession of a loaded prohibited or restricted firearm
Knowledge of unauthorized possession of firearm
Unauthorized possession of a prohibited or restricted firearm
Possession of weapon for dangerous purpose (firearm)
Pointing a firearm
Firearm – Use while committing offence
Careless storage of a firearm
A 23-year-old Barrie woman is charged with:
Accessory after the fact to an indictable offence (attempted murder)
Robbery (cell phone)
A 32-year-old Midland woman is charged with:
Accessory after the fact to an indictable offence (attempted murder)
The investigation is ongoing. Investigators believe additional photos and videos of the incident exist. Anyone who was in the downtown area at the time of the altercation and shooting and captured images or video is asked to come forward. Police are also aware a woman had her cell phone stolen during the incident and would like to speak with her.
The Barrie Police Service would also like to thank members of the public who have already come forward with tips and video evidence, which significantly assisted investigators.
The investigation was led by the Barrie Police Service Major Crime Unit, with assistance from Criminal Investigations Unit, Uniform Patrol, Tactical Support Unit members, and partner policing agencies.
Anyone with additional information is asked to contact the Barrie Police Major Crime Unit at 705-725-7025 ext. 2160, email

title: Be aware of scams during tax season
content:
Fraud Alert
,
With tax season underway, police are reminding residents to be alert to scams involving individuals posing as the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA).
Fraudsters often contact victims by phone, email or text, claiming they owe back taxes or are entitled to a refund. They may use urgent or threatening language and demand immediate payment. Requests for payment by gift cards, cryptocurrency or wire transfer are clear warning signs.
Police remind the public that the CRA does not threaten to arrest or demand payment through gift cards or cryptocurrency.
“The only forms of communication that the CRA utilize is through use of the My CRA login, by text/email notifications advising there is new activity in your My CRA Account, or by registered mail at your last known address,” stated Detective Kris Nicholson with the Barrie Police Service Fraud Unit.
To protect yourself:
Do not share personal or banking information with unsolicited callers.
Do not click on suspicious links or attachments.
Verify any tax-related request by contacting the CRA directly through official channels.
Use strong passwords and enable multi-factor authentication on online accounts.
Anyone who believes they have been targeted or victimized is encouraged to report the incident to police and online with the Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre.
Staying cautious and verifying information can help prevent financial loss during tax season.

title: Internet Child Exploitation Unit charges two men following separate investigations
content:
The Barrie Police Service Internet Child Exploitation Unit has arrested and charged two individuals in separate investigations involving the possession and distribution of child sexual abuse and exploitation material.
On Wed., March 11, officers arrested and charged a 25-year-old male following a two-month investigation initiated after the Internet Child Exploitation Unit received several reports from the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children (NCMEC). The reports indicated suspected uploads of child sexual abuse and exploitation material online.
On February 25, officers executed a search warrant at a residence in Springwater Township. Multiple electronic devices belonging to the accused were seized and later subjected to forensic examination, which confirmed the presence of child sexual abuse and exploitation material.
The accused has been charged with possession, accessing, and distribution of child sexual abuse and exploitation material.
Also on March 11, the Internet Child Exploitation Unit arrested and charged a 53-year-old Barrie man following a separate four-month investigation. This investigation began after the unit received a report from NCMEC regarding the upload of suspected child sexual abuse and exploitation material.
Investigators executed a search warrant in December 2025 at a residence in the city’s south end. Several electronic devices were seized for forensic analysis, which has since revealed more than 100 images of child sexual abuse and exploitation material.
The accused was charged with possession, accessing, and distribution of child sexual abuse and exploitation material.
This project has been made possible by a grant from the Ministry of the Solicitor General.

title: A call for help, not enforcement: Officers find elderly woman trapped for days
content:
When someone stops answering their calls, misses their appointments, or raises concern among family or friends, police are often called to make sure they are safe.
Wellness checks are a critical part of policing, with Barrie Police officers regularly responding to calls involving mental health concerns, personal crises and vulnerable residents in need of support.
In November, police responded to a wellness check for a 90-year-old woman after family members grew concerned when they hadn’t heard from her in several days and learned she had missed a scheduled transit pickup.
When officers arrived at the residence, they found the front door open.
“When we entered the residence, we initially feared the worst,” said Sgt. James Westcott. “We started a search of the home, and eventually we heard faint cries for help.”
The officers located the woman behind a jammed bathroom door, lying in an empty bathtub. “She had fallen into the tub and was unable to move for four days, so our focus was on getting her out safely,” Sgt. Westcott said.
The officers carefully lifted the woman from the tub, wrapped her up for warmth, and stayed by her side until paramedics could arrive and transport her to a local hospital for assessment.
This situation underscores the important role police play in supporting vulnerable residents and ensuring their safety and well-being.
In most wellness cases, officers attend the scene, assess the situation and work to ensure the individual’s immediate safety. Depending on the circumstances, wellness checks may involve connecting individuals with medical care, crisis intervention teams, or community-based support services.
These calls focus on helping people during difficult moments. Officers receive training in crisis response and de-escalation techniques and often work alongside health and community partners to make sure individuals receive the support they need.
Residents are encouraged to contact police if they believe someone may be at risk. Emergencies should be reported by calling 911, while non-urgent concerns can be directed to the non-emergency line at 705-725-7025.
Barrie Police Service offer programs and resources to support vulnerable residents, including
Project Lifesaver
, the
Vulnerable Person Registry
, and
MCRT and CARE
.
We remain committed to supporting residents during difficult moments and encourage anyone with concerns about a person’s safety to reach out for help.

title: Driver with open alcohol containers, cannabis in car blows nearly 2x over legal limit
content:

Traffic

Shortly before midnight on Wed., March 4, two men walking north on Clapperton Street near Dunlop Street East caught the attention of Barrie police officers on general patrol who noticed the pair appeared unsteady on their feet as they approached a parked vehicle.
Police watched as one of the individuals got into the driver’s seat of the vehicle parked along Clapperton Street, while the second individual entered the passenger seat. Officers then positioned their cruiser behind the vehicle, which was idling.
A licence plate check through the Ministry of Transportation database revealed the registered owner of the vehicle had a driver’s licence that had recently expired.
The officers approached the vehicle, and during the interaction, noticed several opened alcoholic beverage containers in the rear seat area of the vehicle.
While speaking with the driver, officers noted signs of impairment, including slurred speech and glossy eyes. The officers also spotted open cannabis packages near the centre console. When asked, the driver admitted to having consumed “a few” drinks.
As a result, the driver was arrested for impaired operation and transported to Barrie Police Service headquarters for further testing where breath samples showed he was nearly 2x over the legal alcohol limit.
A 36-year-old Barrie man was subsequently charged with:
Drive motor vehicle – no licence
Have care or control of a vehicle with cannabis readily available
Operation while impaired
Operation while impaired – blood alcohol concentration (80 plus)
In the past seven days, Barrie police have taken nine drivers into custody for impaired driving.
Impaired driving isn’t just a statistic – it’s a decision with real consequences. Barrie police urge motorists to take a moment to plan your way home. One responsible choice can prevent tragedy.

title: ALPR alerts officer to suspended driver resulting in multiple charges
content:

Upon speaking with the officers, the male denied driving, claiming the vehicle had already been parked. Officers advised him they had observed the vehicle travelling on Anne Street North and entering the lot.
The ALPR system had also captured images confirming the vehicle and licence plate.
Further investigation determined the driver was operating the vehicle while his licence was suspended.
As a result, a 40-year-old Barrie man has been charged with:
Driving while under suspension
Fail to stop when signalled by a police officer
Drive motor vehicle with no valid permit
Operate a motor vehicle without insurance
ALPR technology automatically scans licence plates to confirm permits, vehicle status, and Criminal Code alerts, giving officers real-time alerts that enhance proactive policing efforts.
Police remind motorists that driving while suspended and failing to stop for officers are serious offences. Anyone choosing to get behind the wheel must be licensed, insured, and in full compliance with the law.

title: Fraud Prevention Month: Recognize the signs of a Romance Scam
content:
Fraud Alert
,
Romance scams affect people of all ages and backgrounds, often leaving victims emotionally and financially harmed. These scams typically occur when someone creates a fake online identity to form a relationship with the goal of gaining trust and, eventually, money or personal information.
Grooming Tactics
Romance scams most commonly begin on dating apps, social media platforms, or online forums, but they can also start through email or messaging apps.
Scammers often spend weeks or even months building a connection. They may appear attentive, affectionate, and eager to move the relationship forward quickly.
In many cases, the scammer will avoid meeting in person and may claim to live or work far away, often citing reasons such as military service, overseas employment, or travel restrictions.
Financial Requests
Once trust has been established, requests for money usually follow. These requests can be framed as emergencies, investment opportunities, medical expenses, travel costs, or temporary financial hardship.
Scammers may ask for payment through gift cards, cryptocurrency, wire transfers, or online payment platforms, methods that are difficult to trace or recover.
Requests for secrecy are also common, with victims encouraged not to tell friends or family about the relationship or financial assistance.
Red Flags
There are several warning signs that may indicate a romance scam.
These include someone professing strong feelings very early in the relationship, reluctance or refusal to video chat or meet in person, stories that contain inconsistencies, or pressure to send money quickly.
Requests for personal information, such as copies of identification, banking details, or login credentials, should also raise concern.
Protect Yourself
To protect yourself, it is important to be cautious when interacting with people you have not met in person.
Take time to verify identities, be skeptical of urgent financial requests, and avoid sending money or personal information to someone you only know online.
Talking openly with trusted friends or family members about new online relationships can also help provide perspective and identify red flags.
Report It
If you suspect you may be a target of fraud, or if you have already sent funds, don’t be embarrassed – you’re not alone.
If you have lost money through fraud, you can call the Barrie Police Service Fraud Unit at 705-725-7025, or
.
If you want to report an attempted fraud, or if you need more information, contact the
Canadian Anti-Fraud Centre.

title: Road rage investigation results in slew of charges
content:
,
Traffic
Barrie police officers have laid a slew of charges against a driver involved in a road rage incident in the city’s west end last month.
On Feb. 20, Barrie police received a report about a female motorist travelling westbound on Dunlop Street West when a pickup truck merged from the Highway 400 northbound off-ramp without signaling and proceeded to drive in an erratic and dangerous manner.
Officers launched an investigation into the complaint and obtained video footage of the incident which revealed the pickup truck merged without signalling, cut off the complainant, stopped in the lane, accelerated to block her lane change, tailgated at close range, and made a turning motion toward the complainant’s vehicle in a manner that could have resulted in a serious collision with oncoming traffic.
Fortunately, no collision occurred and no injuries were reported.
Through the investigation officers were able to identify the driver of the pickup truck, who surrendered to police at Barrie Police Headquarters on Sat. Feb. 28.
As a result of the investigation, a 46-year-old man from Essa Township is charged with dangerous operation under the Criminal Code of Canada.
Additionally, he faces several charges under the Highway Traffic Act:
Fail to signal- Lane change
Fail to yield to traffic on through highway
Unnecessary slow driving
Follow too closely
The Barrie Police Service reminds motorists that aggressive and dangerous driving behaviour puts all road users at risk. Incidents of road rage should be reported to police, and drivers are encouraged to provide video evidence when available to aid in the investigation.
Occurrence #CL26000667

title: Police offer safety tips for buying and selling items online after recent thefts
content:
Buying and selling items online is a convenient way to clear out unwanted property and earn extra cash, but there can be risks involved. The Barrie Police Service wants everyone to take their safety into consideration when conducting private transactions after recent thefts.
In both cases, the sellers arranged to meet buyers after listing an iPad for sale online and the devices were stolen while the sellers were distracted during the transactions.
In one incident, a female attended the seller’s residence and appeared to complete an e-transfer payment in exchange for the iPad. Moments later, a male suspect arrived at the door, grabbed the device, and fled with the female suspect to a waiting vehicle driven by a third individual. The sellers later discovered that the e-transfer was fraudulent and no payment had been sent.
Police urge residents to consider their personal safety and take preventative measures when meeting with unknown individuals.
The Barrie Police Service offers its headquarters parking lot as a safe location for residents to complete online transactions:
Location:
110 Fairview Road
Safety Features:
The parking lot provides a secure, public place to meet.
Best Practices:
Conduct transactions during daylight hours and avoid meeting alone whenever possible.
Alternative Option:
If you cannot attend the police station, choose a well-lit, busy, public location.
Residents are encouraged to remain vigilant and trust their instincts.
Ultimately, your safety is not worth the value of any item you are selling. If a situation feels suspicious or escalates, do not attempt to physically prevent a theft or confront suspects. No amount of money or property is worth risking your personal safety. Instead, prioritize your well-being and report the incident to police as soon as possible.
Anyone who believes they have been the victim of a scam or fraudulent transaction is asked to contact the Barrie Police Service non-emergency line at 705-725-7025. In an emergency, always call 911.
Occurrence #CL26000812

title: Arrests made after keen-eyed officers spot suspicious activity
content:
Keen-eyed Barrie police officers on proactive patrol observed two males pulling a large bin full of tools shortly before 2 a.m. in the area of Dunlop Street West and Anne Street on Feb. 26, 2026.
The officers approached the two men on suspicion they were in possession of stolen property and through the investigation learned one of the individuals had an outstanding warrant with another police service. He was arrested on the strength of that warrant.
While continuing their investigation, the officers spotted suspected controlled substance residue inside a glass pipe, resulting in the arrest of the second male for possession. Further search led to the discovery of a prohibited knife and a cannister of Bear Repellent.
The officers then followed the tracks left in the snow from the two men back to a pickup truck on Hart Drive where it’s believed the tools were stolen.
As a result of the investigation, a 39-year-old Barrie man was charged with possession of stolen property obtained by crime under $5,000, possession of a prohibited weapon, and failure to comply with prohibition order.
Additionally, a 38-year-old man is facing charges of possession of stolen property obtained by crime under $5,000.
Occurrence #BA26013013

title: Driver with child passenger blows 3x over legal limit after collision
content:
A Barrie woman is facing multiple criminal charges after a single-vehicle collision in the city’s south end.
Officers were dispatched to an area on North Village Way in Park Place on Wed., Feb. 25, around 6:30 p.m. following reports of a suspected impaired driver who had struck several protective pillars out front of an indoor play facility. The concerned citizens who called police indicated the vehicle had nearly driven into the building’s main entrance.
At the time of the crash, the woman’s five-year-old child was inside the vehicle. Fortunately, no injuries were reported.
The driver attempted to leave the scene, but the significant damage to the vehicle from the collision caused it to stop close by.
Officers arrived quickly and launched an impaired driving investigation. The woman provided breath samples which revealed she was three times over the legal alcohol limit.
The 42-year-old woman from Barrie faces charges of:
Operation while impaired
Operation while impaired – blood alcohol concentration 80-plus
Dangerous operation
She was later released with a future court date.
The child was picked up by a family member at the scene.
Impaired driving continues to pose a significant risk to the community. Choosing to drive while under the influence of alcohol or drugs not only endangers the driver, but also passengers and others on the road. Anyone who suspects an impaired driver is urged to call 911 immediately.
Occurrence #BA26012949

title: Copper wire theft from vacant building results in charges
content:
Two Barrie men are facing multiple charges following a break and enter investigation at a vacant commercial property on Blake Street.
On Feb. 24, 2026, at roughly 3:30 p.m., Barrie Police received a report of suspicious activity at the site of a former restaurant that has been closed since a 2022 fire. A witness reported seeing two individuals leaving the building with what appeared to be bags of copper wire. The witness said the materials were loaded into a vehicle, and one of the males drove off while the other left the area on foot.
Officers quickly located and detained one suspect nearby. Through the investigation, police learned the second suspect had attended a local scrap metal facility to sell the materials. Officers attended the business and less than an hour after the initial call, located and arrested the second individual in the area of Anne Street and John Street.
Police believe the copper wire and other materials were removed from inside the vacant building after the suspects gained entry through a damaged window. An inspection of the property confirmed a window on the north side of the building had been smashed.
Investigators obtained documentation from the scrap yard indicating Once back at Barrie Police Headquarters, officers also seized cash and related paperwork from one of the suspects, which was directly linked to the sale.
As a result of the investigation, two Barrie men, ages 33 and 39, have each been charged with:
Break and Enter with Intent to Commit an Indictable Offence
Possession of Stolen Property under $5,000
Trafficking in Property Obtained by Crime
Additionally, the 39-year-old individual is also charged with Possession of Break-In Instruments and Breach of Probation.

title: Barrie Police Service warns residents about new scam involving caller impersonating an officer
content:
Fraud Alert
,
The Barrie Police Service is warning residents about a new scam involving a male caller claiming to be an officer with the Barrie Police Service contacting victims about suspicious activity with their bank accounts.
In each case, the victims were pressured to provide additional personal and financial information. When they refused, the caller warned that any resulting financial losses would not be covered by their bank if they didn’t cooperate.
In one instance, a victim also received an email containing a fake police badge and identification card in an attempt to make the scam appear legitimate.
The fraudulent badge was poorly produced and contained several obvious red flags, including a crest that does not match the official Barrie Police Service crest. As well, the image was sent from a free email service provider, whereas all Barrie Police Service members have BarriePolice.ca email addresses.
If you are unsure whether a caller is a legitimate Barrie Police member:
Advise the caller that you will hang up and contact the Barrie Police Service directly through the non-emergency line.
Call the official non-emergency number at 705-725-7025 and follow the prompts to be connected to the officer by name.
If the call is legitimate, a Barrie Police Service officer will be easily reachable through official channels.
Barrie Police remind residents to:
Never click on suspicious links or open attachments from unknown email addresses.
Never provide personal or financial information to unknown callers.
Do not be afraid to say no — scammers often create a false sense of urgency and demand secrecy.
If you suspect you may be a target of fraud or are a victim of fraud, call the Barrie Police Service Fraud Unit at 705-725-7025, or
report the fraud online
.
Occurrence # BA26012449

title: Join the Conversation: Barrie Police to Host Two Open House Sessions on how AI and Technology Shape the Future of Policing
content:
The session will be livestreamed through the Barrie Police Service Facebook account:
https://www.facebook.com/barriepoliceservice
The Barrie Police Service (BPS) is inviting members of the community to join us for a Community Conversation Series focused on the use of technology and artificial intelligence (AI) to support crime prevention, investigations, and public safety.
The open house discussions will take place at the Downtown Barrie Library at 6:30 p.m. on March 17 and March 31.
The series will feature a panel of experts presenting both advantages and concerns related to these technologies to ensure a balanced discussion.
The first session on March 17 will focus on new technology and feature panel speakers Ryan Fritsch from the Law Commission of Ontario, Shakir Rahim from the Canadian Civil Liberties Association, and BPS Chief Rich Johnston.
Ryan Fritsch, Legal Counsel, Law Commission of Ontario
Ryan Fritsch is legal counsel with the Law Commission of Ontario. He leads law reform projects considering the impact of artificial intelligence in Ontario’s criminal justice system; the impact of technology on workplace surveillance; and modernizing consumer protection in the digital marketplace. Ryan is a frequent public speaker and lecturer, with over 10 years’ experience teaching law students at the Osgoode Hall Law School and University of Windsor. Ryan is also a doctoral candidate at Osgoode Hall researching the data broker industry in Canada.
Shakir Rahim, Director, Criminal Justice Program, Canadian Civil Liberties Association
Shakir Rahim is a lawyer and Director of the Criminal Justice Program at the Canadian Civil Liberties Association. He oversees and executes advocacy strategies to protect and develop Charter rights in criminal law, hold police accountable, and defend prisoners’ rights. His work includes strategic litigation management, public outreach and education, and engagement with policymakers in a range of fora. Prior to joining CCLA, Shakir practiced criminal law and acted on significant public interest matters, such as co-leading the campaign to establish the Independent Civilian Review into Missing Person Investigations concerning the Toronto Police Service.
Barrie Police Chief Rich Johnston
Chief Rich Johnston was sworn in as the 10th Chief of the Barrie Police Service on December 22, 2022.Chief Johnston has been with the Barrie Police Service since 1998, serving in a variety of roles throughout the Service, including Uniform Patrol, Tactical Support, Operational Support, and Investigative Services. He holds an Honours Bachelor of Arts Degree in Philosophy from Queen’s University, a Master’s in Leadership Studies from the University of Guelph and a Master’s degree in Applied Criminology and Police Management from the University of Cambridge (United Kingdom).Chief Johnston joined the senior command in 2018 as an Inspector, and was appointed as Deputy Chief in 2021. In 2022, Chief Johnston received the Ontario Association of Chiefs of Police Excellence in Leadership Award and was inducted into the Evidence-Based Policing Hall of Fame at the Centre for Evidence-Based Crime Policy at George Mason University.
The second session on March 31 will concentrate on facial matching technology, with panelists from Ontario Tech University.
Dr. Peter Lewis – Associate Professor of Computer Science & Canada Research Chair in Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence
Dr. Lewis’s research advances both foundational and applied aspects of trustworthy, reflective, and socially intelligent systems. Drawing on extensive experience applying Artificial Intelligence (AI) commercially, he is interested in where AI meets society, and how to help that relationship work well. Dr. Lewis is also an Honorary Senior Research Fellow in the School of Computer Science at the University of Birmingham, UK.
Dr. Christopher O’Connor – Associate Professor of Criminology and Justice
Dr. O’Connor has expertise in emerging and disruptive technologies related to policing and energy. He is building evidence-based research around police use of new technologies and social media to guide law enforcement decisions.
Dr. Matt Bland - Chief Operating Officer, Society of Evidence-Based Policing (UK)
Matt Bland is the Chief Operating Officer at the Society of Evidence Based Policing (SEBP). He was a crime analyst in UK policing for 15 years during which he studied on the Cambridge Institute of Criminology’s MSt in Applied Criminology and Police Management course. Subsequently he undertook his PhD research at the Institute and joined the faculty as a full-time lecture in 2019. Matt is also a Visiting Senior Fellow in Policing at the University of Suffolk and a scholar at the Jerry Lee Institute for Experimental Criminology.
Dr. Andrea Slane, Professor of Legal Studies
Dr. Slane’s research focuses on privacy, data protection, and the variety of legal regimes that protect people from both individual and commercial wrongdoing online, over digital devices and smart technologies.
These sessions will provide the public an opportunity to learn about the proposed technologies, ask questions, and share their concerns and suggestions in an open and transparent environment.
Public engagement plays a key role in shaping this process. A public survey will be available ahead of the open house sessions to gather community perceptions and concerns, which will be addressed during the sessions.
“Technology can be a very effective tool in law enforcement and public safety,” said Barrie Police Chief Rich Johnston. “Avoiding these advancements would be a disservice to our community. We are dedicated to being transparent about the tools we use and earning the public’s trust through open and respectful conversations.”
The sessions will be live streamed for anyone unable to join in person. Please visit BarriePolice.ca for more information on the sessions, including a link to the survey and the livestream which will be available in the coming weeks.  The survey is also available here:
https://www.surveymonkey.com/r/BPSTechnology

Dates: March 17 & March 31, 2026
Time: 6:30 p.m.
(Doors open at 6:00 p.m., join us for light refreshments)

title: Did you know? Refusing a breath sample can result in criminal charges
content:
,
Traffic
We all know impaired driving is a criminal offence. What some motorists may not realize is that you can also face the same serious consequences for refusing to provide a breath sample when asked to do so during a lawful traffic stop.
Did you know that under Canada’s mandatory alcohol screening law, police officers can demand a roadside breath sample from any driver during a traffic stop without needing reasonable suspicion the driver has alcohol in their system? This law is designed to deter impaired driving and increase road safety.
If an officer makes a lawful demand and the driver refuses or fails to comply without a reasonable excuse, that refusal itself is a criminal offence. Mandatory alcohol screening allows officers to use an approved screening device at the roadside. The test is quick and non-invasive.
The penalties for refusing to provide a breath sample are the same as those for impaired driving. Upon conviction, a first offence carries a mandatory minimum fine of $2,000, along with a criminal record and a driving prohibition.
Simply put, if an officer asks you to provide a breath sample during a traffic stop, you are required by law to do so. The goal of the legislation is prevention. Impaired driving is still one of the leading criminal causes of death in Canada.
The Barrie Police Service continues to urge drivers to plan ahead, arrange a safe ride home, and never get behind the wheel after consuming alcohol or drugs. It’s just not worth the risk.

title: Fail-to-remain investigation escalates into search and rescue operation
content:
On Fri., Feb. 20, Barrie Police assisted the Ontario Provincial Police following a motor vehicle collision on Highway 400, just south of Mapleview Drive, around 5:30 p.m. involving two male suspects who had fled the scene.
It was determined the suspects ran into a wooded area, making their way eastbound into a swampy green space located between McKay Road and Lockhart Road.
The area, filled with waist-deep snow and a mud bottom, created a treacherous environment for the fleeing suspects. As Barrie Police officers arrived on scene to support the investigation, their focus quickly shifted from a routine arrest. K9 Officers, along with Tactical Support Unit officers, began tracking the suspects through the waist-deep snow, making their way into the difficult terrain.
At this point, officers assessed the situation and recognized the dire risk the suspects were in. Both men were dressed inadequately for the harsh winter conditions, and it became clear that they were in immediate danger of hypothermia. With concerns for their safety, it was determined that the mission would pivot to a search and rescue.
A Barrie Police RPAS (remotely piloted aircraft system/drone) was deployed to assist, but the inclement weather hindered visibility, making it difficult to capture clear footage of the suspects.
Around 6:30 p.m., the first suspect, a 21-year-old man, was located stuck in the snow. He was arrested and carried back to the highway by officers. Given the severity of the first suspect’s condition, officers feared the second suspect, dressed similarly, would be in even greater jeopardy.
At roughly 7 p.m., the second suspect, a 20-year-old man, was found buried in the snow, immobilized in the swamp. Officers worked painstakingly to extricate him from the snow and carried him back toward the highway, where they were met by Barrie Fire Services, who deployed a rescue sled.
“This was an excellent example of two police services working together and not only arresting two suspects for criminal offences, but also saving their lives,” stated Sgt. James Westcott.
Both suspects were taken into custody and transported for medical evaluation.
Occurrence #BA26011908

title: Downtown Patrol Leads to Arrest of Driver Nearly 3x Over Legal Alcohol Limit
content:
A Barrie police officer on patrol in the city’s downtown conducted a wellness check on an individual asleep in a parked vehicle with the engine running that resulted in an arrest.
On Thurs., Feb. 19, around 9:30 p.m., the officer was in the area of Mulcaster Street when she observed a vehicle with its rear hatch partially open. A lone individual was seated in the driver’s seat and appeared to be asleep.
The officer approached and made contact with the driver. During the interaction, the officer observed signs of impairment, including slow and slurred speech and difficulty responding to instructions. At one point while speaking with the officer, the man reached for an open container of alcohol within the vehicle.
He was placed under arrest and transported to the Barrie Police Headquarters for testing with a qualified breath technician. The breath samples provided indicated the individual was nearly three times the legal alcohol limit.
As a result of the investigation, a 61-year-old man from Barrie has been charged with:
Operation while impaired – blood alcohol concentration (80 plus)
Operation while impaired – alcohol and drugs
The vehicle was impounded, and his driver’s licence was suspended in accordance with provincial legislation.
Impaired driving remains a leading cause of criminal death in Canada and poses a significant risk to public safety. Over the past week, Barrie police officers have made half a dozen impaired driving arrests.
Police remind residents that even if a vehicle is stationary, a person can still be charged with impaired operation or care and control if they are found to be impaired while in a position to operate it.
Anyone who observes suspected impaired driving is encouraged to call 911.
Occurrence #BA26011756

title: Did you know? Officers use cell phones on the job – and it’s not to check social media
content:
Residents who see a police officer on a cellular device after a call for service may wonder what’s happening behind the screen. The Barrie Police Service wants to assure the public our officers aren’t scrolling the latest trends or texting about dinner plans – they’re hard at work.
In fact, those devices have become an important tool on the job.
Did you know many frontline police officers now rely on secure digital note-taking applications to document information in real time? Instead of juggling a traditional paper notebook, officers can record observations and investigative details directly into a protected system while still on scene.
While it may look similar to sending a text message, what’s actually happening is often the careful documentation of information. Capturing details immediately helps preserve timelines, improve accuracy, and ensure key facts are recorded properly.
“We are not on social media or streaming services,” stated Staff Sgt. Toni Talarico. “We are using our phones to write police notes digitally, label body camera videos, write reports and fill out forms, view evidence sent by the public on Evidence.com, and many more work-related tasks.”
Modern technology allows officers to spend less time returning to the station to rewrite notes and more time visible and engaged in the community.
Public trust is strengthened through transparency, and sometimes that starts with explaining the everyday tools of the job. So, the next time you notice an officer on their cell phone, just know they’re very much hard at work – because every detail matters when it comes to keeping the community safe.

title: Armed man arrested in city’s downtown
content:
On Sun., Feb. 15, shortly after 7 a.m., a Barrie police officer on patrol in the city’s downtown area recognized and identified a man with an active warrant. After noticing the officer, the man tried to evade him by ducking behind a building and keeping his head down.
When the officer caught up to the individual and attempted to make the arrest, he resisted but was quickly restrained and taken into custody.
During the arrest process, the officer discovered the man was in possession of two concealed weapons, which were hidden beneath his clothing.
The 28-year-old Barrie man now faces multiple charges including resisting arrest, carrying concealed weapons, failure to comply with a release order, and failure to comply with a probation order.
This incident highlights the ongoing efforts of Barrie police to uphold community safety and hold individuals accountable for unlawful behavior.
Occurrence #BA26010669

title: Impaired Driver Stopped After Heading Wrong Way on Highway 400 On-Ramp
content:
,
Traffic
An observant officer prevented a potentially serious collision after spotting a vehicle travelling the wrong way on a ramp to Highway 400 in Barrie.
Shortly before 11 p.m. on Tues., Feb. 17, a Barrie police officer on patrol in the area of Bayfield Street observed a vehicle heading northbound onto the Highway 400 southbound ramp, placing oncoming motorists at significant risk. The officer immediately acted and conducted a traffic stop before the vehicle merged onto the highway in the wrong direction.
During the interaction with the driver, the officer noted obvious signs of impairment. As a result of the investigation, the driver was arrested and transported to Barrie Police Headquarters for further testing.
A 25-year-old man from Bradford has been charged with operation while impaired – alcohol and operation while impaired – blood alcohol concentration (80 plus).
He was further charged with:
Driving motor vehicle with open container of liquor
Drive motor vehicle – no licence
Dangerous operation
This incident could have ended very differently. The officer’s quick actions helped to safely resolve what could have been an incredibly dangerous situation that put that driver’s life in jeopardy, along with other motorists and responding police officers.
Impaired driving charges carry serious consequences, including an immediate licence suspension, vehicle impoundment, significant fines and a criminal record upon conviction.
Occurrence# BA26011324

title: Armed break-and-enter at Barrie residence under investigation
content:
On Fri., Feb. 13, Barrie police were alerted to a reported break-in at a home on Woodfern Court in the area of Holly Meadow Road and Kenwell Crescent involving three males armed with weapons.
Officers received the call just before 3 a.m. about three suspects who shattered a rear glass door to gain entry to the residence. The homeowner advised police that two of the suspects were armed with handguns and a third carried what appeared to be a hammer or small hatchet.
The suspects fled the home, seemingly empty-handed, through the front door prior to police arrival.
No one was physically injured.
Officers quickly established a perimeter, deploying K9 and a drone, to search for the suspects but were unsuccessful in locating them. Police canvassed the area and have obtained video surveillance footage to aid in the investigation.
The suspects are described as three young Black males. The first suspect wore white shoes, the second wore black and white shoes, and the third wore dark shoes. All three were dressed in black hoodies and masks.
The investigation is ongoing.
Investigators are asking residents in the area of Holly Meadow Road between Woodfern Court and Sundew Drive to check their home security footage for any suspicious individuals or vehicles between 2:30 a.m. and 3 a.m. on Feb. 13.
Anyone with information is encouraged to contact Det. Const. Colameco at
[email protected]
.

title: Did you know? There’s more to policing than responding to crime
content:
When many people think about policing, they often envision criminal investigations, arrests, and emergency calls involving crime. While that work is an important part of what we do, the reality is it’s only one part of the picture.
Every day, a significant portion of calls to the Barrie Police Service involve matters unrelated to criminal offenses. Officers frequently respond to mental health crises, wellness checks, missing persons reports, family and neighbour disputes, traffic collisions, and general community safety concerns.
In many of these instances, enforcement isn’t the primary objective. Instead, the focus is to ensure public safety and prevent situations from escalating. Barrie police officers dedicate a significant amount of time helping people who are in distress or crisis. This includes checking on an individual who has not been heard from by their family, helping someone through a mental health crisis, or making sure a vulnerable person is safe and connected with the right supports.
For example, in January of this year, officers were called to the Allandale Transit Terminal to assist an 84-year-old man who had arrived from Toronto and appeared confused. Const. Matt McGill and Const. Caleb Wells took time speaking with the senior and discovered he had mistakenly boarded the wrong bus to get home, intending to travel north of Sudbury. The officers managed to track down a friend of the man in Elmvale who could provide a safe place to stay for the night. Despite a blistering snowstorm, the officers loaded the man’s walker, canes, and belongings and drove him to his friend’s house for the night.
Officers are also frequently called to manage disputes, which are often emotionally charged situations. Their role in these situations is to de-escalate tensions, maintain the safety of everyone involved, and prevent conflicts from worsening.
Collisions, road hazards, and impaired or unsafe driving is also a key component of policing. It’s not all about handing out tickets – officers aim to prevent injury and keep everyone safe on the roads.
Not every call results in charges or arrests. A large part of policing is about prevention, support and harm reduction.

title: Barrie police launch Project LIMA to improve pedestrian and cyclist safety
content:
Barrie police launched Project LIMA, a traffic safety initiative aimed at reducing collisions involving pedestrians and cyclists in high-risk areas across the city. This effort responds to recent incidents and addresses the increased challenges posed by winter conditions, such as slick roads and limited visibility at intersections, which can make navigating busy streets more hazardous.
The initiative combines targeted Highway Traffic Act enforcement with public education, focusing on locations identified through collision data and traffic pattern analysis. The project is a response to rising concerns around visibility, driver behaviour, and safety at busy intersections.
Project LIMA will concentrate enforcement and education efforts across the city, including in several focused safety zones such as the Bayfield Street corridor, the downtown core around Dunlop Street, and the Yonge Street and Big Bay Point Road area. Barrie Police front-line officers, alongside members of the Traffic Unit, will play a key role in delivering these targeted initiatives to enhance road safety for all, while still addressing traffic concerns throughout the city.
Education efforts will target drivers, cyclists, and pedestrians, with the goal of improving awareness and reducing preventable collisions.
Drivers can actively contribute to safer roads by ensuring their vehicles are completely cleared of all snow and ice, headlights are working properly, watching their speeds, adjusting driving according to the weather conditions and remaining alert avoiding distractions such as cell phones and other devices that take your eyes off the roads, including while stopped at intersections.
Cyclists are reminded to use lights and reflective gear and obey traffic laws, while pedestrians are advised to stay visible and use designated crossings.
“Project LIMA is about using data to focus our efforts where the risk is highest,” said Barrie Police Chief Rich Johnston. “By combining enforcement with education, we’re working to improve safety for everyone who uses our roads, particularly pedestrians and cyclists.”
This initiative reflects a proactive approach to road safety, with an emphasis on prevention and shared responsibility.

title: Driver 2.5x over legal alcohol limit charged following multi-vehicle collision

Dangerous operation

Operation while impaired – blood alcohol concentration (80 plus)
Failure to surrender driver’s licence
Use plate not authorized for vehicle

title: Police remind motorists to adjust driving according to road conditions this winter
content:
The Barrie Police Service is reminding motorists to adjust their driving according to the road conditions as winter weather can change rapidly and without warning.
Our officers are responding to a significant number of collisions caused by drivers travelling too fast for conditions, following too closely, or failing to adjust their driving to snow-covered, icy, or slushy road surfaces. Even when main roads appear clear, bridges and side streets can be slippery and unpredictable.
On Fri., Feb. 6, officers responded to a two-vehicle collision at the intersection of Huronia Road and Big Bay Point Road. The incident occurred when a passenger car believed to have been travelling too fast for the slick conditions lost control and struck another vehicle.
The driver of the second vehicle suffered minor injuries. The 22-year-old driver of the passenger car has been charged with careless driving, an offense that carries significant penalties upon conviction.
In winter conditions, even small mistakes can result in vehicles leaving the roadway, sliding into intersections, or colliding with other vehicles.
Drivers are reminded to slow down, increase following distance, and allow extra travel time during winter weather. Proper winter tires, clear windows and mirrors, and functioning lights also play an important role in reducing collision risk.
Police also remind drivers that speed limits are for ideal conditions and that drivers need to adjust their speed according to road and weather conditions.
If you must head out, clear all snow and ice from your vehicle, give yourself extra time to get where you’re going, pack your patience, keep your eyes on the road and your hands on the wheel.

title: GPS tracking devices found attached to vehicles by suspected auto thieves
content:
Barrie police are warning the public after GPS tracking devices were recently found attached to vehicles parked in a lot in the city’s north end by suspected auto thieves.
Officers received the call on Wed., Feb. 4, after two vehicle owners discovered the devices attached under the hood in an area easily accessed from outside the vehicle near the windshield. The vehicles involved were newer-model Toyota vehicles, specifically a Toyota Tundra and a Toyota Highlander, and had been parked at Royal Victoria Regional Health Centre.
Police reviewed video footage from Mon., Feb. 2 and observed an individual on foot stopping near both vehicles and reaching under both hoods before walking off.
Investigators believe more vehicles may have had tracking devices placed on them, and suspect the devices are being used to monitor vehicle locations and movements to determine an opportunity to steal them.
Both vehicle owners reported receiving repeated notifications on their mobile devices indicating a tracking device was nearby. However, it’s important to note that not all cell phones will trigger a notification of this sort and vehicle owners shouldn’t rely solely on phone alerts to detect a tracker.
Vehicle owners are encouraged to remain vigilant and check their vehicles – particularly newer models – for anything unusual, including underneath the hood, in areas that could be accessed without opening the vehicle.
A reference photo has been provided showing a newer Toyota vehicle with the area circled where trackers have been located. While the devices in these incidents were on Toyota vehicles, police stress that any make or model could potentially be targeted.
Reporting suspicious activity can help prevent vehicle thefts and assist investigators in identifying those responsible. Anyone who discovers a suspicious device is asked not to touch it and to contact the police. If you believe a crime is in progress, call 911.
