title: Did you know? You can be charged for distracted driving even while stopped at a red light
content:
,
Traffic
The Barrie Police Service wants motorists aware of what hands-free means when behind the wheel to keep everyone safe on the roads.
Did you know that while you’re driving, whether stopped in traffic, at a red light, or a stop sign, it is illegal under the Highway Traffic Act to use a hand-held device to text or dial unless contacting emergency services. This includes cell phones, tablets, other communication or entertainment devices and display screens visible to the driver that are unrelated to driving, such as a computer or video.
Distracted driving is as dangerous as impaired driving because it takes your attention off the road putting you and other motorists at risk.
Det. Const. Ron Hunt with the Barrie Police Service Traffic Unit emphasizes how significant the consequences of distracted driving can be.
“Imagine being notified by a police officer that your loved one has passed away in a motor vehicle collision. The officer turns over the cell phone to their loved one only to discover they died texting a friend a simple message like, ‘I’m running late,’ or ‘Can’t wait to see you’.”
Hands-free driving means motorists should not be scrolling, typing, or touching their device while behind the wheel for any reason.
“The only exception is when the device is on handsfree mode or while making a 911 call,” Det. Const. Hunt explains.
Did you know the penalties for distracted driving are significant and increase with each conviction.
For fully licensed drivers:
First offence: The fine starts at $615 and can be as high as $1,000, three demerit points, and a three-day licence suspension upon conviction
Second offence: A fine up to $2,000, six demerit points, and a seven-day licence suspension upon conviction
Third or subsequent offence: A fine up to $3,000, six demerit points, and a 30-day licence suspension upon conviction
And the penalties are increased for novice drivers (G1, G2, M1, M2), including the possibility of having your licence cancelled for repeat offences.
Barrie police remind drivers that distracted driving is a leading cause of serious collisions. Stay focused while driving with your eyes on the road and not on your device to help keep everyone safe.

title: Slick road conditions result in several collisions involving emergency vehicles
content:
,
Traffic
On Tues., Jan. .27, slick road conditions resulted in Barrie police cruisers along with emergency vehicles being struck in incidents across the city.
Fortunately, no serious injuries were reported.
These incidents serve as a reminder of the critical safety measures motorists should follow when approaching traffic stops or emergency scenes on the roads.
Slow Down:
Reduce your speed when approaching any traffic stop, collision, or emergency scene.
Move Over:
When it’s safe, change lanes away from the stopped vehicle or emergency responders to give them space. This isn’t just a recommendation, it’s the law.
Stay Alert:
Pay close attention to the road, emergency lights, and any instructions from officers or signage. Avoid using mobile devices to record video or snap pictures while passing the scene as this could put you and others at risk.
These precautions help protect officers who are performing their duties and ensure everyone’s safety on the road.
The safety of both first responders and the public is paramount. The Barrie Police Service thanks the public for its cooperation and commitment to safe driving practices.

title: Wanted man arrested after trying to flee officers, ramming cruisers
content:

Operation while prohibited under the Criminal Code

Dangerous operation
Assault peace officer with a weapon (x2)

Possession of a Scheduled 1 Substance for the purpose of trafficking – opioid
Possession of a Schedule 1 Substance – opioid

The passenger in the suspect vehicle was initially detained but later released unconditionally.

title: Understanding the signs of human trafficking
content:
The Barrie Police Service is aware of recent content posted to social media regarding alleged tagging of vehicles in parking lots, and the concern that this is being done by suspected human traffickers.
We want to assure the public there have been no reports or evidence of human traffickers marking vehicles in parking lots to target potential victims.
Barrie police want to reassure the public that it is incredibly rare for a stranger to randomly target another stranger for human trafficking. Human traffickers primarily focus on vulnerable individuals and typically engage in a process called ‘grooming.’ This process involves manipulation, emotional abuse, addiction, threats, violence, isolation, and control.
According to statistics by
Human Trafficking in Canada – Ally Global Foundation
– 91 per cent of human trafficking victims know their trafficker.
Some signs to be aware of include:
Sudden changes in behavior, including fear, anxiety, depression or hypervigilance
Withdrawal from friends, family, or usual activities
Signs of physical abuse or neglect
Possession of unexplained gifts, money, or phones
The Barrie Police Service’s Human Trafficking Unit focuses on identifying, investigating, and supporting victims of human trafficking. The goal of these specialized officers is to end human trafficking, often referred to as ‘modern day slavery,’ in our community.
We encourage anyone who sees suspicious persons or activity to report it to police by calling the non-emergency line at 705-725-7025 or visiting our
website to report
vandalism to a vehicle. In the event of an emergency, please call 911 immediately.

title: 2026-2029 Strategic Plan
content:
The plan was developed through collaboration with the Barrie Police Services Board, Barrie Police Service members, community partners, and residents. Creating this plan took teamwork, and we thank everyone who gave feedback or took part in surveys and discussions.
“Our Strategic Plan serves as our road map. As a public safety organization, we are dedicated to policing by consent – acting with objectivity and transparency while treating everyone we encounter with dignity and respect. Our aim is to continue to strengthen police legitimacy. We want our community to not only feel safe but be truly safe and secure in Barrie.” – Barrie Police Chief Rich Johnston
The BPS is happy to report the Community Safety and Satisfaction Survey revealed most respondents are satisfied with our Service, including officer approachability, visibility, and efficiency. Additionally, crime and harm in the city have gone down since the launch of our last Strategic Plan (2023-2025).
Our new plan continues to focus on keeping Barrie one of the safest communities in Canada.
As we look to the future in this growing city, the Service strives to Prevent and Address Crime, Advance Community Safety through Collaboration and Communication, and focus on Organizational and Member Wellness, as outlined in the Strategic Plan.
For more information on our strategic plan, we encourage you to visit our website at
2026-2029 Strategic Plan | Barrie Police Service
.

title: Man, 63, charged in child exploitation investigation
content:
On Jan. 20, 2026, the Barrie Internet Child Exploitation Unit (ICE) arrested and charged a 63-year-old male from Springwater Township with possession of child sexual abuse and exploitation material and accessing child sexual abuse and exploitation material after a three-month investigation.
The ICE Unit received a report from the National Centre of Missing and Exploited Children concerning the upload of child sexual abuse and exploitation material.

online

On Thurs., Jan. 15, 2026, Barrie police responded to a call about a suspicious person which resulted in a series of charges for two people, plus the seizure of drugs believed to be destined for our streets.

title: When to call 911 as non-emergency calls continue to strain resources
content:
Barrie Police Service is reminding residents to only use 911 for true emergencies, and if an accidental call to 911 is placed, to stay on the line. This reminder comes following a continued increase in 911 hang-up and non-emergency calls being placed to the emergency line.
In 2025, Barrie police received 36,539 calls for service to 911. Of those, 17,325 were accidental.
911 is a critical, life-saving service designed to respond to situations where lives, safety, or property are in immediate danger. When the line is tied up with non-urgent calls or accidental dialing, it can delay response to real emergencies where every second matters.
Examples of situations where 911 should be called include:
A crime in progress
Someone’s life or safety is in immediate danger
A serious medical emergency
A fire or suspected fire
A serious collision, especially if there are injuries or road hazards
A person with a weapon or immediate threat to the public
For non-emergency police matters, residents are encouraged to use the Barrie Police Service non-emergency line at 705-725-7025 or the
online reporting tool
.
Examples of situations that do not require 911 include:
Reporting a crime that already happened and is no longer in progress
Noise complaints
Parking complaints
Property damage with no suspect present
General questions or requests for information
Non-urgent neighbour disputes
Police also continue to see a high number of accidental 911 calls, often caused by cell phones in pockets, purses, allowing children to play with phones or by pressing the emergency call feature on a device.
Barrie Police Service reminds the public that if you accidentally call 911, do not hang up. Stay on the line and tell the call-taker the call was a mistake. Hanging up requires dispatchers to call back or, in some cases, send officers to confirm everyone is safe, which uses valuable resources.
If you cannot stay on the line, a communications operator will initially call back and if unsuccessful in reaching the caller, a text will follow to confirm that no emergency exists.
Using the right line for the right situation helps ensure emergency resources are available for those who truly need them.
Barrie Police Service thanks the community for their cooperation and for helping keep emergency lines available for critical calls.

title: Armed robbery under investigation
content:
On Wed., Jan. 14, officers responded to a report of a robbery that occurred at a plaza on Cundles Road East during what was meant to be a Facebook Marketplace transaction.
Two individuals attempting to sell a high-end computer graphics card valued at approximately $5,000 arranged to meet a buyer in a public setting.
While the sellers were interacting with one of the believed-to-be buyers, a second individual arrived and attempted to steal the graphics card from them.
A struggle ensued and the second individual brandished a firearm and pointed it at the victims.
One of the victims was subsequently assaulted. He was taken to a local hospital for treatment of his injuries.
Both suspects fled the store on foot emptyhanded heading westbound.
Suspect #1 is described as a Black male with shoulder-length red/orange dreadlocks, a slim build, roughly 5’6”, believed to be in his late teens to early 20s. He wore a black hooded coat with grey underarms, a black medical face mask, light grey skinny Nike track pants, and black shoes with white soles.
Suspect #2 is described as a Black male, with a medium/athletic build, roughly 6’, believed to be in his mid to late 20s. He wore a black hooded sweater under a black shinny puffy coat, a black face covering, black skinny track pants, and while and pink running shoes.
A black Volkswagen Golf was seen fleeing the area at a high rate of speed; however, it is not confirmed if it was involved at this time.
The investigation is ongoing.
Occurrence # BA26003268

title: Tips to prevent vehicle theft amid surge
content:
The Barrie Police Service has recently seen a notable increase in vehicle thefts. In light of this surge, police are urging residents to take extra precautions to protect their vehicles from being stolen.
In December 2025 alone, 20 vehicles were reported stolen in the City of Barrie. Toyotas, particularly Toyota Tundras, were the most targeted vehicles.
While locking your vehicle doors is essential, it may not always be enough to deter more determined thieves. Whenever possible, park your vehicle in a locked garage with the windows up and doors securely locked. Avoid leaving your vehicle keys near front doors or windows, as thieves can use technology to intercept key fob signals.
To enhance security, police recommend using additional deterrents such as steering wheel locks, automatic engine shut-off devices (pictured below) or tracking systems that can help locate your vehicle if it is stolen.
Installing video surveillance, like a doorbell camera, along with motion-sensing exterior lights, can also aid investigations if your vehicle goes missing.
Furthermore, placing key fobs inside a Faraday pouch can add an extra layer of protection as the pouch can block signals from devices commonly used by thieves to capture key fob data.
The Barrie Police Service understands the impact vehicle theft can have and urges vehicle owners to adopt these preventative measures.
Residents can easily obtain a free Faraday pouch by visiting the front desk at Barrie Police Headquarters on Fairview Drive. Proof of residence in Barrie, such as a driver’s license or tax bill, is required.
Report any suspicious persons or activity at barriepolice.ca or by calling the Barrie Police Service non-emergent line at 705-725-7025.

Yesterday afternoon, just before 5:00 p.m., the Barrie Police Service was made aware of a suspected impaired driver who was entering the north end of Barrie from Springwater Township.

title: Can you help bring Autumn home?
content:
Autumn
,
Today is a sombre day, as June 12, marks the day one year ago that the Barrie Police Service first received information that Autumn Shaganash was missing. It symbolizes a significant mark on an ongoing investigative timeline that detectives from the Service’s Major Crime Unit hope will one day conclude when Autumn is reunited with her family.
In the past year, Autumn Shaganash has been subject of numerous searches, a recent electronic billboard and transit bus campaign and on February 8 of this year, the Barrie Police Service’s Board authorized a reward of up to $50,000 for information confirming her current whereabouts.
Detectives have followed up countless leads and tips, but none of them have help to determine the whereabouts of this missing Barrie resident who was 26-years old at the time of her disappearance. When last seen on Saturday June 10, between 10:00 a.m. and noon hour near Sunnidale Park, she was wearing tan-coloured leggings, slip on Puma sandals, black hoodie and carrying a black and tan purse.
Video surveillance from a residential home in the area has captured her walking with a male, each of them carrying a ski. The male was walking just ahead of her as they made their way towards the Barrie waterfront to watch an Air Show. Autumn never made it; as the friend, who has been interviewed by detectives, states at one point he looked back and Autumn was no longer following him. She just vanished into thin air, without a sound or cry for help, she was gone and never seen or heard from again.
“Our Major Crime Unit and our detectives have never lost sight of our focus of reuniting Autumn with her family” states Sgt. Brett Carleton who is leading this complex and very challenging investigation.  Sgt. Carleton adds, “If you are someone who has information that can assist this investigation, I encourage you to call Barrie Police now – let’s work together to bring Autum home.”
Occurrence #
:
BA23037609
Prepared by
:
Corporate Communications Coordinator Peter Leon

title: Protected: Police investigate multiple break and enters
Buses and billboards carry important message
content:
Autumn
,
On February 8, 2024, Barrie Police Chief Rich Johnston announced, on behalf of the Barrie Police Services Board, a $50,000 reward for information confirming the whereabouts of 27-year-old Autumn Shaganash.
The hope of reuniting Autumn with her family remains the primary focus of detectives from the Service’s Major Crime Unit who have actively been investigating her disappearance.
Autumn was reported missing to the Barrie Police Service on Monday, June 12, 2023, at 1:30 p.m. She was last seen leaving a family member’s home on Friday, June 9, near Burton Avenue and Franks Way at 11:00 p.m.
Between 9:30-9:45 a.m. Saturday morning (June 10), a text message was received from Autumn asking to be picked up. Only three minutes passed and when her sister tried to respond to the message, her phone went to voicemail and appeared to have been turned off.
Autumn was a regular user of social media and since that Saturday morning text, no one has heard from her.
Autumn was last seen in the area of Sunnidale Park (close to the intersection of Sunnidale Road and Letitia Street) between 10:00 a.m. and noon hour and was wearing a black hooded sweatshirt, tan-coloured leggings, slip on Puma sandals and was carrying a black and tan coloured purse. She was making her way to the Barrie Waterfront to watch an Air Show with a male friend who was walking slightly ahead of her when she vanished. At the same time, a walk for ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis) was taking place in the park and being a pleasant Saturday in June, the park was a busy place. Police have made public appeals for surveillance video and any potential witnesses who may have seen Autumn on that day to come forward.
In what is an investigative first for the Barrie Police Service, and commencing Monday, April 1, 2024, and continuing for the entire month of April, five Barrie Transit buses will carry a message to all corners of the city reminding residents and transit users that the investigation into Autumn’s disappearance remains active, ongoing and that the Barrie Police are using all available means to reunite her with her family.
In addition to the buses, four digital billboards in the City of Barrie will also carry a message that is hoped to generate the necessary tips that will bring resolution to this investigation that stymied detectives for over 9 months.
The Barrie Police Service wish to acknowledge the incredible support of the community in this ongoing investigation, Barrie Transit and the partnership with Pattison Outdoor Advertising who are responsible for the transit king posters and billboards depicting Autumn’s image.
If you have information that you can share with police, please call the Barrie Police Service Major Crime Unit at 705-725-7025, extension 2160.

Barrie Police arrested three men on Thursday, May 7, 2026, accused of attempted robbery in an industrial area in the city’s south end.

title: Roofing repair company attempts to defraud Barrie senior
content:
Fraud Alert
,
Investigators with the Barrie Police Service Fraud Unit are warning residents about a recent incident involving a roofing repair company that attempted to defraud an elderly Barrie man out of thousands of dollars.
On March 16, 2026, police were called to a local financial institution after concerns were raised about a possible fraud involving an 84-year-old man attempting to withdraw a significant amount of money for alleged roof repairs. Police learned individuals had reportedly damaged the man’s chimney and were demanding payment for repairs while threatening to report the damage if he refused to pay.
Through the investigation, the senior explained he was at his home in the area of Nelson and Codrington streets when he heard loud banging coming from his roof. Shortly after, two males dressed like contractors knocked on his door and offered their services to repair his chimney.
The victim told police the contractors threatened he would lose his house if he didn’t pay to have the chimney fixed. A contract listed the company as Ontario Roof Repairs & Masonry.
Investigators are hoping to identify the parties involved in the attempted fraud. Anyone with information is encouraged to report it with the Barrie Police Service Fraud Unit
online
, or with the
Canadian Anti-Fraud Unit.
To remain anonymous, contact Crime Stoppers at 1-800-222-8477.
Members of the community are reminded to be cautious of unsolicited contractors, avoid making rushed payments under pressure, and consider seeking a second opinion before agreeing to costly home repairs.

title: Don’t wait 24 hours to report someone missing
content:
The Barrie Police Service is clearing up some common misconceptions surrounding reporting a missing person, including when to notify police.
Do I have to wait 24 hours before reporting someone missing?
No. There is no waiting period. If someone is missing and their absence is out of character, or you are concerned for their safety or well-being, you should contact police immediately. Seconds matter in missing persons cases, especially those involving children or vulnerable adults.
On April 9, 2026, shortly after 9 a.m., police received a report of a missing vulnerable man who had not returned to his retirement residence after last being seen leaving the evening prior. Police were alerted to the fact he did not have access to a phone, money, or tracking device, and had a history of removing a GPS safety bracelet.
Officers immediately began investigating, which included reviewing surveillance footage to confirm his last known direction of travel, conducting a search of the residence and surrounding area, and deploying a remotely piloted aircraft system (drone) to assist in checking nearby parks and wooded areas.
Roughly an hour after receiving the initial call, police located the 64-year-old man in good physical health wandering the streets in his slippers. He appeared confused and said he was lost and did not know where he lived. He was also unable to account for his whereabouts overnight.
Because he has a history of removing his safety tracking bracelet, officers implemented a safety plan to help reduce risk and support his well-being.
This occurrence highlights how quickly situations involving vulnerable persons can change and supports ongoing efforts to raise awareness about reporting missing persons without delay.
Should I report an adult missing?
Yes. While adults are free to make their own decisions, Barrie police investigate all missing person reports to ensure the individual is safe and not at risk. The goal is always to confirm the person’s well-being.
What information will police need?
Officers will ask for a physical description, last known location, what the person was wearing, their routines, known associates, and whether there are any medical, mental health, or safety concerns. The more detailed the information, the better police can focus their efforts.
What steps do police take to find a missing person?
Every case is assessed individually. Depending on the circumstances, police may check hospitals and shelters, speak with family, friends, employers, or schools, review available surveillance footage, and conduct area searches. Additional police units or partner agencies may also be involved when needed.
Why do police post about missing people on social media?
When appropriate, police will use social media in a missing person investigation as a tool to appeal to the public for help. One of the most valuable assets in a missing person investigation is a recent and unfiltered photograph. We recommend keeping updated, unaltered photos of loved ones, as they can significantly aid in locating someone quickly.
Is reporting a missing person wasting police time?
No. If you are genuinely concerned about someone’s safety, you should always call. Police would rather assess the situation early than be notified too late.
Are most missing people found?
Yes. Most missing persons are located safe and well, often within a short period of time. However, every report is treated seriously, and officers continue working until the person is located or their well-being is confirmed.
What should I do if I have information about a missing person?
Anyone with concerns about a missing person, or information that could help locate someone, is urged to contact police immediately.
Barrie Police Service offers programs and resources to support vulnerable residents, including
Project Lifesaver
, the
Vulnerable Person Registry
, and
MCRT and CARE
.
Occurrence #BA26022293

title: Barrie woman charged with arson after Essa Road basement fire
content:
Barrie Police officers arrested and charged a 59-year-old woman less than an hour after a suspicious fire in the basement of a residence on Essa Road.

The two occupants escaped the residence unharmed. The basement sustained moderate damage. Fire officials subsequently advised police the fire appeared to have been intentionally set.
An officer who had noticed a vehicle outside the address at the time of the fire then located the registered owner. The officer attended a residence in the area of Essa Road and Loggers Run where the woman answered the door with what appeared to be significant burn injuries to her hands.
She was taken into custody at 4:45 a.m. and transported to a local hospital for medical attention. Following her release from the hospital, she was brought to Barrie Police Headquarters and held for a bail hearing.
The accused is charged with:
Arson – Damage to Property
Arson – Disregard for Human Life (x2)
The Ontario Fire Marshal was notified about the suspected arson but is not attending.

title: 2025 Annual Report Highlights Community Safety and Proactive Policing
content:
The Barrie Police Service is proud to release its 2025 Annual Report, highlighting a year of proactive policing, community partnerships, and continued efforts to support safety and well-being across the city.
This report highlights the final year of the 2023-2025 Strategic Plan by reflecting on our progress, while also looking ahead to future priorities for policing in Barrie.
Throughout 2025, Barrie Police members continued to focus on proactive policing and community engagement.  Officer-generated occurrences increased in 2025 and accounted for more than 22 per cent of calls for service, while the community-reported harm score decreased from the year prior.
The Service’s Communications Centre continued to exceed national standards, answering a remarkable 98.5 per cent of all 911 calls in under 15 seconds. Use of the Text-to-911 service also increased significantly over the past two years.
A major focus in 2025 was the enhanced Downtown City Guardian Program, delivered in partnership with the City of Barrie. Working alongside the Community Safety and Well-Being Engagement Team, Special Constables provided a visible presence throughout the downtown core with a focus on engagement, well-being checks, and connecting people with available supports and services.
The report also highlights several initiatives introduced in 2025 to support both members and the community, including the launch of 30 Forward, the addition of a second wellness coordinator position, and the introduction of a victim liaison officer role to better support victims of crime as they navigate the justice system.
“Policing is about people first and this report showcases the work our members are doing every day to support the community in a meaningful way. Whether it’s responding to emergencies or supporting someone through difficult situations, there is a lot of important work happening behind the scenes. I’m incredibly proud of our members and grateful for the continued support from the community and our partners.” – Chief Rich Johnston
Please view the 2025 Annual Report online at
BarriePolice.ca/Newsroom/Publications
.

title: Driver served with impaired charges at fast-food eatery
content:
Barrie Police served a 21-year-old man with impaired driving charges after finding him asleep at the wheel in a fast-food eatery parking lot.
Officers were called to the Bayfield Street location on Sunday morning around 7:15 after a concerned caller reported a vehicle idling for more than two hours. When police arrived, they found a man slumped over in the driver’s seat with his foot positioned near the accelerator pedal.
After officers woke him, the engine was shut off. Police noted the driver appeared disoriented, with glassy eyes and slurred speech, and detected a strong odour of alcohol.
An Approved Screening Device test was administered at the scene, which resulted in a fail. The driver was subsequently arrested for impaired operation and while being escorted to the police cruiser, he struggled to maintain his balance. He was transported to police headquarters for further testing, where a qualified breath technician confirmed he was nearly twice the legal alcohol limit, resulting in a second charge of impaired driving over 80.
The accused was also handed a licence suspension, and the vehicle was towed away as per the Highway Traffic Act.
Barrie Police remind motorists that ‘sleeping it off’ behind the wheel of a parked or idling vehicle can still lead to impaired driving charges. If you are in care or control of a vehicle after consuming alcohol or drugs, you can be found legally impaired and face serious consequences.
Plan ahead. Stay safe.

title: Four teens arrested after series of incidents in Leacock Drive area
content:
Four youths are facing charges after a series of incidents Sunday evening involving threats with a weapon and thefts from businesses in the Leacock Drive area.
Police learned that earlier in the evening a large group of teenagers approached some youths playing at Lampman Lane Park. One male brandished a knife and threatened a 15-year-old male and a 14-year-old male in separate incidents. No one was injured.
The group then made its way to a nearby plaza on Leacock Drive, where items were stolen from two businesses. When a business owner confronted the group, the same male from the park produced a knife again and made threats, prompting the owner to retreat into the store.
While leaving the area, the suspect made another threat aimed at a witness while pointing to the knife in his pocket.
Officers located two suspects nearby, while two others were found a short time later after fleeing into a wooded area.
As a result of the investigation, a 16-year-old male from Barrie is charged with:
Robbery with violence
Assault with weapon (x3)
Theft under $5,000
Possession of a weapon for a dangerous purpose
Possession of a weapon contrary to a court order
Failure to comply with sentence
A second 16-year-old male from Barrie is charged with:
Theft under $5,000
Mischief under $5,000
Failure to comply with undertaking
Additionally, a 13-year-old male is charged with uttering threats to cause bodily harm, and a 14-year-old male is charged with theft under $5,000.
The identities of those involved are protected under the Youth Criminal Justice Act.
Occurrence #BA26032378

title: Road rage incident leads to charge for Springwater man
content:
A 56-year-old man from Springwater Township has been charged in connection with a road rage incident involving a group of motorcyclists.
On May 13, 2026, Barrie Police launched an investigation after receiving an online traffic complaint, along with video evidence, of the incident that occurred around 2:30 p.m. the day before.
The video showed a pickup truck swerving toward a motorcycle travelling along Bayfield Street near Hanmer Street. A verbal interaction then took place between another rider and the pickup truck driver, after which the truck maneuvered dangerously toward that motorcycle.
No injuries were reported.
Members of the Barrie Police Traffic Unit identified the pickup truck driver, who has since been charged with dangerous operation.
Anyone with information is asked to email the investigating officer at
[email protected]
.
Barrie Police review and actively investigate complaints
reported online
, along with submitted video evidence, as part of ongoing road safety enforcement.
CL26002550

title: Project Starter results in record stolen vehicle recovery for Barrie Police Service and South Simcoe Police Service
content:
A joint Barrie–South Simcoe police investigation has resulted in the largest stolen vehicle recovery in the history of the local Services, marking a significant breakthrough in the fight against organized auto theft.
As part of a complex and challenging three-month investigation, working alongside the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) and
Équité Association
, 40 stolen vehicles were identified and recovered domestically and abroad. Following referrals from Barrie Police, CBSA officers were able to identify shipping containers in Montreal and the Greater Toronto Area holding 19 stolen vehicles. In addition, CBSA Intelligence were able to identify more shipping containers overseas resulting in the recovery of 21 stolen vehicles in the ports of Bahamas, Spain, and Ghana as part of Project Starter.
On Friday, April 24, 2026, members of the Prevent Auto Thefts (PAT) unit made an arrest in Brampton, Ont. in connection with the investigation. A 31-year-old Brampton man is charged with trafficking property obtained by crime and possession of property obtained by crime over $5,000.
The recovered vehicles include Honda CRVs, Toyota Tundras, Lexus RXs, F150s, and one Lamborghini, among others. In total, the value of the recovered vehicles is estimated to be well over $3 million. The vehicles were reported stolen from across Ontario, including Simcoe County, York Region, Toronto, Peel, Halton, and the Niagara Region. The recovery of these stolen vehicles is a major step forward in addressing the growing issue of vehicle theft nationwide.
“This investigation is the direct result of a $1.8 million grant awarded to the Barrie-South Simcoe Police Services by the Ontario government in May 2024.  This grant allowed for the extensive resources necessary, including the addition of a crime analyst who played an instrumental role in the investigation.” – Barrie Police Chief Rich Johnston
Barrie Police would also like to extend thanks to York Regional Police and the RCMP, which work with Équité Association on behalf of the property and casualty insurance industry to repatriate the vehicles to Canada.
Our members remain committed to investigating and dismantling organized crime networks involved in auto thefts.
Anyone with information about auto theft is encouraged to contact Barrie Police at 705-725-7025 or Crime Stoppers anonymously at 1-800-222-8477 (TIPS) or
online
.
Additionally, anyone with information about suspicious cross-border activity should contact the
CBSA Border Watch Line
at 1-888-502-9060.
Occurrence #RM26000255
Quotes
“Thanks to Government of Ontario grant funding through the Ministry of the Solicitor General, we were able to partner with the Barrie Police Service and allocate the specialized resources needed to support this investigation. This support directly strengthened our ability to respond and protect our communities more effectively. I also want to recognize the dedicated work of the Prevent Auto Thefts Unit and the support of our policing partners, including York Regional Police and Équité Association, who work with us and other police services. By working together, we can detect, disrupt, and dismantle organized crime networks, creating safer communities for everyone.”
– South Simcoe Police Chief John Van Dyke
“The CBSA acts on 100 per cent of referrals from the police to help stop stolen vehicles from leaving the country. The Barrie Police Service’s early engagement for this investigation allowed the CBSA to intercept and recover stolen vehicles destined for export. As a result of our collaborative efforts with law enforcement partners like the Barrie Police Service, we continue to curb auto theft in Canada and disrupt organized crime.”
– Lisa Janes, Regional Director General, CBSA Greater Toronto Area Region
“The Barrie Police Service together with the South Simcoe Police and partner agencies are to be commended on the tremendous success of Project Starter. This is a great example of how government investments, collaborative efforts and public-private partnerships are having a significant impact on combatting vehicle crimes in Canada. The criminal networks that are at the root of these financially motivated crimes seek to profit from the $900 million worth of vehicles stolen in Canada each year. Équité Association is proud to partner with the Barrie Police Service, South Simcoe Police Service, the CBSA and other agencies across the country to hold criminals accountable and protect Canadian communities.”
– Bryan Gast, National Vice President, Intelligence & Investigations, Équité Association
FAQS
Q:
What is Project Starter?
A:
Project Starter is a collaborated effort between Barrie Police Service, South Simcoe Police, the CBSA, and Équité Association to combat rising auto thefts, maintain public safety, and disrupt organized crime networks.
Q:
What was the CBSA’s role?
A:
CBSA worked alongside the Barrie Police Service and South Simcoe Police Service to identify shipping containers suspected of transporting stolen vehicles. The CBSA examined and intercepted the shipping containers suspected of containing stolen vehicles destined for export.
Q:
What is Équité Association?
A:
Équité Association is a national non-profit organization that collaborates with law enforcement, government, and insurance companies to combat rising auto theft through coordinated investigations, providing expert vehicle examinations and investigations using data analysis to identify theft patterns.
Q:
Who pays for the vehicles to be returned to Canada?
A:
The cost of repatriation is covered by the owners of the stolen vehicle which is generally the insurance company or the finance company.
Q:
What happens to the vehicles now?
A:
Recovered vehicles are handed over to insurance companies.
Q:
Will the registered owners be notified their stolen vehicle has been recovered?
A:
Yes. Barrie Police will make every effort to notify the registered owners that their vehicle has been recovered. We appreciate that in some cases, personal items may still be within the vehicle, and the owners may wish to retrieve those items.
Q:
How do auto thefts affect the public?
A:
Auto thefts impact real people and communities. When someone’s vehicle is stolen it creates an unsettled feeling for many in their own neighbourhood. On a broader level, frequent thefts contribute to rising insurance costs right across the province. It is not a victimless crime.
Q:
How can vehicle owners protect themselves?
A:
Owners can reduce their risk by ensuring their vehicles are locked, parking in well-lit areas or locked garages, using anti-theft devices, keeping key fobs in Faraday pouches/boxes, and reporting suspicious activity to police.
